content.text:
Here are the results for your search query:

**"Personas as a Way to Model Truthfulness in Language Models"** is a paper published at EMNLP 2024. Note that the first author's name is **Nitish Joshi** (not "Nitarshan").

The paper is authored by Nitish Joshi, Javier Rando, Abulhair Saparov, Najoung Kim, and He He, and was published in the Proceedings of the 2024 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing, pages 6346–6359, Miami, Florida, USA, by the Association for Computational Linguistics.
The authors are affiliated with New York University, ETH Zurich, and Boston University.

The paper presents an explanation for why LMs appear to know the truth despite not being trained with truth labels. The authors hypothesize that the pretraining data is generated by groups of (un)truthful agents whose outputs share common features, forming (un)truthful personas. By training on this data, LMs can infer and represent the persona in their activation space, allowing the model to separate truth from falsehoods and control the truthfulness of its generation.

The authors show evidence via two observations: (1) they can probe whether a model's answer will be truthful before it is generated; (2) finetuning a model on a set of facts improves its truthfulness on unseen topics.

Using arithmetic as a synthetic environment, they show that structures of the pretraining data are crucial for the model to infer the truthful persona. Overall, the findings suggest that models can exploit hierarchical structures in the data to learn abstract concepts like truthfulness.
